learning_objectives: Understand cloud service models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS
Compare major cloud providers: AWS, Azure, GCP
Identify when cloud is and is not appropriate for a workload

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing transformed software deployment from buying servers to renting compute by the second. This course teaches you to architect, deploy, and operate applications on cloud infrastructure, focusing on AWS as the primary platform.
key_concepts: Cloud characteristics: on-demand, broad network access, resource pooling, elasticity, measured service
Service models: IaaS (EC2), PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk), SaaS (Gmail)
Deployment models: public, private, hybrid
Shared responsibility model: provider secures infrastructure, you secure your application
Cost models: pay-per-use, reserved instances, spot instances
lecture_notes.demonstration: Launch an EC2 instance, SSH into it, deploy a simple web application, and examine the AWS billing dashboard to understand cost implications

review.value: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure (physical data centers, hypervisors, network); you secure your configurations, data, access controls, and application code. Many cloud breaches result from customer misconfiguration, not provider failure
The ability to automatically scale resources up when demand increases and down when it decreases; this turns fixed capital expense into variable operational expense proportional to actual usage

learning_objectives: Explain how virtualization and containers work at a systems level

lecture_notes.introduction: Containers are the standard deployment unit for cloud applications. Understanding the difference between VMs and containers, and knowing how to containerize your applications, is a non-negotiable skill for cloud engineering.
key_concepts: Hypervisor-based virtualization: full OS per VM, hardware-level isolation
Containers: shared kernel, process-level isolation, lightweight
Docker: Dockerfile, images, containers, registries
Docker Compose: multi-container applications
Kubernetes: pods, services, deployments, autoscaling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Containerize a Node.js web app with Docker, push the image to ECR, then deploy it to a Kubernetes cluster on EKS with autoscaling based on CPU utilization

review.value: VMs include a full OS (GB-sized, minutes to start); containers share the host kernel (MB-sized, seconds to start). Containers provide process isolation, not hardware isolation, so they are less secure but much more efficient
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes; a pod runs one or more tightly-coupled containers that share network and storage. Deployments manage pod replicas and rolling updates

learning_objectives: Build serverless functions with AWS Lambda
Design event-driven architectures using SQS, SNS, and EventBridge
Understand the trade-offs of serverless vs container-based deployment

lecture_notes.introduction: Serverless computing abstracts away all infrastructure management. You write functions, define triggers, and pay only for execution time. Event-driven architecture connects serverless functions into scalable, loosely-coupled systems.
key_concepts: AWS Lambda: function-as-a-service, triggers, execution model
Cold start: first invocation latency, provisioned concurrency
Event sources: API Gateway, S3, SQS, DynamoDB Streams
Message queues (SQS) and pub/sub (SNS) for decoupling
Serverless trade-offs: no server management but limited execution time and state
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a serverless image processing pipeline: S3 upload triggers Lambda, which resizes the image and stores metadata in DynamoDB, sending a notification via SNS
setup_requirements: AWS account with Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, SNS configured
AWS SAM CLI for local testing

review.value: The first invocation of a Lambda function after idle time requires loading the runtime and your code, adding 100ms-10s of latency depending on language and package size; provisioned concurrency pre-warms instances to eliminate this
Components communicate through events rather than direct calls; the image uploader does not know or care about the resizer. This enables independent scaling, deployment, and failure isolation

learning_objectives: Choose the right cloud storage service for different data patterns
Deploy and manage cloud database services (RDS, DynamoDB)
Implement data backup and disaster recovery strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud providers offer dozens of storage and database services, each optimized for different access patterns. Choosing the wrong service leads to poor performance and high costs. This session teaches you to match data requirements to the right service.
key_concepts: Object storage (S3): unstructured data, unlimited scale, 11 nines durability
Block storage (EBS): attached to EC2 instances, low-latency random access
Relational databases (RDS): managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, Aurora
NoSQL databases (DynamoDB): key-value and document, single-digit millisecond latency
Caching (ElastiCache): Redis and Memcached for read-heavy workloads
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy a PostgreSQL RDS instance, configure automated backups and read replicas, then set up DynamoDB for session storage with on-demand capacity
setup_requirements: AWS account with RDS and DynamoDB access

review.value: RDS for relational data with complex queries and transactions; DynamoDB for high-throughput key-value access with predictable performance. DynamoDB scales horizontally with no limit; RDS scales vertically with a cap on instance size

S3 stores data across multiple facilities with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability; losing an object in S3 is statistically less likely than being struck by lightning. This makes it the default choice for any data that must not be lost

class_schedule.topic: Cloud Security and Infrastructure as Code
learning_objectives: Implement IAM policies following the principle of least privilege
Automate security controls and compliance checks

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud misconfiguration is the leading cause of cloud security breaches. Infrastructure as code (IaC) ensures your cloud environment is defined in version-controlled, reviewable, and repeatable code, eliminating manual configuration drift.
key_concepts: IAM: users, roles, policies, least privilege principle
Terraform: declarative infrastructure definition, plan, apply, state management
Security groups and network ACLs for network-level access control
Secrets management: AWS Secrets Manager, never hardcode credentials
Compliance automation: AWS Config rules, CloudTrail logging
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write Terraform configuration to deploy a VPC with public and private subnets, security groups, an EC2 instance, and an RDS instance with least-privilege IAM roles

review.value: Every IAM principal should have only the permissions required for its specific function and nothing more; overly broad policies (especially admin access) are the most common root cause of cloud security incidents

IaC provides version history (who changed what, when), peer review (PR-based infrastructure changes), repeatability (identical environments for dev/staging/prod), and disaster recovery (recreate infrastructure from code)

questions.question: Explain the difference between horizontal scaling and vertical scaling. Which is preferred in cloud architectures and why?
questions.answer: Vertical scaling (scale up) adds more CPU/RAM to an existing server; it is simple but has a hardware ceiling and creates a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling (scale out) adds more servers behind a load balancer; it has no theoretical limit and provides redundancy. Cloud architectures prefer horizontal scaling because cloud instances are commodities designed to be added and removed dynamically, and distributing load across multiple instances provides both scalability and fault tolerance.

rubric.key: Identifies horizontal as preferred in cloud
Explains fault tolerance and scalability benefits

questions.question: Write a Dockerfile for a Python Flask web application that installs dependencies from requirements.txt and runs the app on port 5000.
questions.answer:
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 5000
CMD ["python", "app.py"]

rubric.key: Copies and installs requirements before app code (layer caching)

questions.question: Design a serverless architecture for a URL shortener service. Describe which AWS services you would use for each component and how they connect.
questions.answer: API Gateway: receives HTTP requests (POST to create short URL, GET to redirect). Lambda (create): generates a unique short code, stores the mapping in DynamoDB, returns the short URL. Lambda (redirect): looks up the short code in DynamoDB, returns a 301 redirect to the original URL. DynamoDB: stores short_code (partition key) -> original_url mapping with TTL for expiration. CloudFront: CDN in front of API Gateway for low-latency global access and caching of redirect responses. Route 53: custom domain for the short URLs. CloudWatch: monitoring and logging for all Lambda invocations.

rubric.key: Correct service selection for each component
Appropriate use of DynamoDB for key-value lookup

questions.question: Explain why the following IAM policy is dangerous and how to fix it: {"Effect":"Allow","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}
questions.answer: This policy grants full access to every AWS service and every resource in the account. It violates the principle of least privilege, meaning if the credentials are compromised, the attacker has complete control. Fix: Restrict Action to only the specific API calls needed (e.g., s3:GetObject, dynamodb:PutItem) and restrict Resource to only the specific ARNs of resources the principal needs to access (e.g., a specific S3 bucket or DynamoDB table).

rubric.key: Identifies the policy grants unrestricted access
Explains the risk of credential compromise
Provides correct fix with specific actions and resources

major_assignments.title: Cloud-Native Application Deployment
major_assignments.description: Design, deploy, and operate a cloud-native application on AWS using infrastructure as code. The application must include containerized services, managed database, autoscaling, monitoring, and proper security controls, all defined in Terraform.
objectives: Deploy a multi-tier application using containers and managed services
Define all infrastructure using Terraform with proper state management
Follow security best practices including least-privilege IAM and encryption
deliverables: Terraform configuration files for all infrastructure
Architecture diagram showing all components and their interactions
Operations runbook covering deployment, scaling, monitoring, and incident response

criteria: Multi-tier design with proper network segmentation
Appropriate service selection for each component
Autoscaling configured with sensible thresholds
Architecture diagram is complete and accurate
All infrastructure defined in Terraform (no manual configuration)
Terraform state is stored remotely with locking
Variables and modules used for reusability
Plan output reviewed before applying
IAM follows least privilege for all roles
Database is in a private subnet with no public access
All data encrypted at rest and in transit
Secrets managed through Secrets Manager, not hardcoded
CloudWatch dashboards and alarms configured
Runbook covers common operational procedures
Deployment process is automated and repeatable
Backup and recovery procedures documented and tested
Well-architected cloud-native application deployment
Complete IaC with Terraform following best practices
Security controls implementing defense in depth
Operational readiness with monitoring and runbook

midterm_examination.description: Written exam on cloud services and containers

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all cloud topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of cloud architectures is encouraged. All Terraform code and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Never share AWS credentials or access keys.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need an AWS Free Tier account, Docker, Terraform, and kubectl installed. Lab credits are provided for AWS usage beyond free tier.
